coredata.dc:title: Fluctuation analysis and experimental evaluation of an inductor-less battery charge equalization circuit with a CMOS cross-coupled structure
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: 2022 The 3rd International Conference on Power Engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Eguchi, Kei
Nakashima, Daigo
coredata.dc:description: A lithium-ion battery for mobile devices obtains a desired voltage by connecting a plurality of battery cells in series. However, the variation in the voltage of each battery cell shortens the lifetime of the battery. For this reason, a battery charge equalization circuit called a battery equalizer (BE) is commonly used. Among others, an inductor-less switched-capacitor (SC) BE is one of the most promising circuits, because it enables low cost, easy control, low EMI, and IC integration. However, to the best of our knowledge, there has been no research on the effects of element variation in inductor-less SCBEs. In this paper, the effect of circuit element variation is investigated regarding the inductor-less SCBE. Specifically, we use Monte-Carlo simulations to clarify the effects of circuit element variation in an inductor-less SCBE with a CMOS cross-coupled structure. Furthermore, the feasibility of the inductor-less SCBE is confirmed by experiments on a laboratory prototype circuit.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluctuation analysis
Battery charge equalization circuits
Battery equalizers
Switched capacitor techniques
Inductor-less circuits

Confirmation experiment of cell voltage averaging

ce:dochead.ce:textfn: 2022 The 3rd International Conference on Power Engineering (ICPE 2022), Science and Engineering Institute, China Chapter, December 09–11, Sanya, Hainan Province, China

head.ce:title: Fluctuation analysis and experimental evaluation of an inductor-less battery charge equalization circuit with a CMOS cross-coupled structure

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Information Electronics, Fukuoka Institute of Technology, 3-30-1 Wajirohigashi, Higashi-ku, Fukuoka, 811-0295, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Information Electronics, Fukuoka Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Information Electronics, Fukuoka Institute of Technology, 3-30-1 Wajirohigashi, Higashi-ku, Fukuoka, 811-0295 Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A lithium-ion battery for mobile devices obtains a desired voltage by connecting a plurality of battery cells in series. However, the variation in the voltage of each battery cell shortens the lifetime of the battery. For this reason, a battery charge equalization circuit called a battery equalizer (BE) is commonly used. Among others, an inductor-less switched-capacitor (SC) BE is one of the most promising circuits, because it enables low cost, easy control, low EMI, and IC integration. However, to the best of our knowledge, there has been no research on the effects of element variation in inductor-less SCBEs. In this paper, the effect of circuit element variation is investigated regarding the inductor-less SCBE. Specifically, we use Monte-Carlo simulations to clarify the effects of circuit element variation in an inductor-less SCBE with a CMOS cross-coupled structure. Furthermore, the feasibility of the inductor-less SCBE is confirmed by experiments on a laboratory prototype circuit.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, secondary batteries, such as lithium-ion batteries, nickel cadmium battery, and nickel hydrogen battery, are used in various electronic devices including mobile devices. In the lithium-ion battery for mobile devices, a plurality of battery cells is connected in series to obtain a desired voltage. However, due to variations in battery cells, variations in the voltage of each battery cell occur. The imbalance of battery cell voltages concentrates stress on some cells and degrades the battery. For this reason, a battery charge equalization circuit called a battery equalizer (BE) [1–9] is commonly used, because the voltage variation shortens battery lifetime.

Although the BEs with magnetic components [1,2] can achieve efficient equalization capabilities, a compact and lightweight, the BE with no magnetic component is desirable for future mobile devices that are becoming more highly integrated. For this reason, several studies have been conducted on the development of small and light BE. For example, as a traditional inductor-less BE, the battery equalizer designed by switched capacitor (SC) techniques [3] is well-known. The traditional SCBE [3] consists of only 8 MOSFETs and 3 capacitors. Therefore, the traditional SCBE can achieve easy control, simple structure, and low hardware cost. However, the traditional SCBE has the disadvantages of low power efficiency and slow voltage averaging speed. To improve the drawbacks of the traditional SCBE, Baughman et al. proposed a double-tiered SCBE [4]. By adding a second layer of capacitors, the double-tiered SCBE can increase the voltage averaging speed. However, the extra capacitor increases hardware cost and energy loss. Following this research, Ye et al. developed SCBE with a parallel structure [5]. Although the hardware cost of Ye’s SCBE is almost the same as that of the traditional SCBE, a capacitor voltage averaging path is built across all battery cells. However, this technique is less efficiency, because the current passes through two capacitors during the averaging process. The star and delta structured-SCBEs were proposed by Shang et al. [6,7]. The star-structured SCBE [6] can achieve high averaging efficiency and speed without major changes in hardware. However, averaging efficiency and capacity are limited due to the increased number of capacitors passed through during averaging. On the other hand, the delta-structured SCBE [7] has a single capacitor that passes through for energy transfer, resulting in higher average acceleration and higher efficiency. However, the number of required capacitors increases, resulting in a larger circuit size and higher cost. Furthermore, these SCBEs require gate driver circuits to drive the gate terminals of the MOSFETs. To overcome these problems, Yang et al. devised a symmetric SCBE [8]. The symmetrical SCBE consists only of diodes and capacitors and operates with two-phase pulses. Therefore, it is possible to effectively average the voltage of the battery cells without requiring gate driver circuits. Furthermore, the symmetrical SCBE has no magnetic components, allowing for low EMI and IC integration. However, the threshold voltage of diodes reduces the cell voltage. Following this study, we developed an inductor-less SCBE with a CMOS cross-coupled structure [9]. In this SCBE, the CMOS cross-coupled structure can avoid the threshold voltage drop caused by the diodes. As the above-mentioned research survey shows, several inductor-less SCBEs have been proposed in past studies. However, to the best of our knowledge, there has been no research on the effects of element variation in inductor-less SCBEs.

In this paper, we clarify the influence of the variation of the circuit elements in the inductor-less SCBE. Specifically, by using Monte-Carlo simulations, the fluctuation analysis is performed to examine the effect of element variation on the inductor-less SCVE with a CMOS cross-coupled structure. Furthermore, the feasibility of the inductor-less SCBE is confirmed by experiments.

The paper is organized in 5 different sections. Section 2 describe the circuit topology of the inductor-less SCBE with a CMOS cross-coupled structure. Following Section 3, the fluctuation analysis using Monte-Carlo simulations is performed regarding the inductor-less SCBE. Section 4 demonstrates the experimental result of the laboratory prototype of the inductor-less SCBE with a CMOS cross-coupled structure. Finally, Section 5 summarizes the results and findings of this research.

Fig. 1 illustrates an example of the proposed inductor-less SCBE with four battery cells [9]. Unlike the conventional inductor-less SCBEs, the proposed SCBE requires no diode by employing a CMOS cross-coupled structure. Therefore, it is possible to avoid the voltage drop of the battery cells due to the diode threshold voltage. By driving the capacitor
C
i
(
i = 1
, …, 8) with two-phase pulses
V
i n 1
and
V
i n 2
, the proposed SCBE equalizes the voltage of the battery cells, where the amplitude of
V
i n 1
and
V
i n 2
is ±
V
i n
/ 2
. Namely, the equivalent circuits of the proposed SCBE are expressed by Fig. 2, where
R
o n
denotes the on-resistance of MOSFETs. Nomenclature is given in Appendix. In this theoretical analysis, the proposed SCBE is expressed in a four-port mode [10] to clarify the circuit characteristics.

To investigate how variations in values of circuit elements affect the characteristics of SCBE, Monte-Carlo simulations are performed under the conditions that
V
i n
= 3 . 7
V,
T = 1
μ s,
R
o n
= 0 . 1
Ω ,
C
i
= 3 . 3
μ F, and
BT
k
is assumed as the capacitor
C
B T k
= 470
μ
F. In this simulation, we investigate the influence of variations in the capacitance and the threshold voltage of MOSFETs. In the Monte-Carlo simulation, the number of simulation executions is 1000 times and the variation ranges in the capacitance and threshold voltage were set to ± 20% and 0.5 – 1.5 V, respectively.

Fig. 3 shows the voltage
V
B T k
(
k = 1
, …, 4) when the value of
C
i
(
i = 1
, …, 8) changes by ± 20%. Table 1 summarizes the simulation results of the effect on the cell voltage when changing
C
i
. Table 2 describes the simulated result of the effect on power efficiency when changing
C
i
. Since the waveforms overlap 1000 times for all cell voltages, we can see from Fig. 3 that the effect of variation in
C
i
is very small.

Fig. 4 shows the voltage
V
B T k
when the value of
C
B T k
(
k = 1
, …, 4) changes by ± 20%. Table 3 summarizes the simulation results of the effect on the cell voltage when changing
C
B T k
. Table 4 describes the simulated result of the effect on power efficiency when changing
C
B T k
. As Fig. 4 shows, the effect of variation in
C
B T k
as well as
C
i
is also very small.

Fig. 5 shows the voltage
V
B T k
when the threshold voltage of MOSFETs changes from 0.5 – 1.5 V. Table 5 summarizes the simulation results of the effect on the cell voltage when changing threshold voltage. Table 6 describes the simulated result of the effect on power efficiency when changing threshold voltage. Unlike capacitance variation, the threshold voltage variation has a large effect on the cell voltage. In particular, the difference between the maximum and minimum cell voltages is about 0.142 V. Figs. 6 and 7 illustrate the histograms of cell voltage and power efficiency, respectively. In Fig. 6, the cell voltages vary according to a normal distribution with a median value of approximately 3.29 V across all cells. On the other hand, the power efficiency varies according to a normal distribution with a median value of approximately 88.7% in Fig. 7.

To confirm the validity of the circuit topology, the conventional SCBE [8] and the proposed SCBE were assembled and their operation was confirmed. First, to compare the operation of the conventional SCBE and the proposed SCBE, several experiments were conducted by connecting small capacitors instead of batteries. Next, to justify the feasibility of the proposed SCBE with a CMOS cross-coupled structure, the operation of the proposed SCBE using the battery LiFePO4 was confirmed experimentally.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative experiment between the conventional SCBE and the proposed SCBE
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 8 depicts the experimental circuits used in the comparative experiment. The circuit components for assembling the experimental circuits are shown in Table 7. Fig. 9 demonstrates the measured
V
B T i
of the experimental circuits. In this experiment, the oscilloscope MSOX2014 A, the dc power supplies PA18-5B, and PMC18-1 A were used to measure
V
B T i
. In the experimental results, the cell voltage of the conventional SCBE is about 3.0 V. Whereas, the cell voltage of the proposed SCBE is about 3.57 V. Table 8 summarizes the measured
V
B T i
. Due to the threshold voltage of diodes, the cell voltage of the conventional SCBE is reduced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confirmation experiment of cell voltage averaging
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 10 shows the experimental circuit of the proposed SCBE using the battery LiFePO4. The circuit components for assembling the experimental circuits are shown in Table 9. In this experiment,
V
B T i
was measured with the same instruments as described in Section 4.1. Table 10 summarizes the measured
V
B T i
of the proposed SCBE. As can be seen from Table 10, the average voltage of the battery cells is about 3.17 V in steady state. In initial state, the standard deviation of
V
B T i
was 5.34 × 10−2 V. On the other hand, the standard deviation of
V
B T i
was 1.09 × 10−2 V in steady state. Furthermore, the maximum error of
V
B T i
decreased from 2.49% to 0.481%. From these experimental results, the proposed SCBE can equalize the battery cell voltages.

In this paper, the effect of circuit element variation has been investigated regarding the inductor-less SCBE with a CMOS cross-coupled structure. The result of Monte-Carlo simulations revealed that capacitor variation does not significantly affect the battery cell voltage. Whereas, variation in threshold voltage had a large effect on battery cell voltage. Concretely, the difference between the maximum and minimum cell voltages was about 0.142 V and the standard deviation of the variation in cell voltages was about 2.53 × 10−2 V. From these results, it was found that the threshold voltage variation significantly affects the fabrication of the proposed SCBE. Furthermore, the experiments using the laboratory prototype were performed to confirm the feasibility of the proposed topology. In the experiment, the maximum error of the battery cell decreased from 2.49% to 0.481%. From the experimental result, the proposed SCBE can equalize the battery cell voltages. A future task is to integrate the proposed SCBE on an IC chip and evaluate the circuit characteristics through experiments.

Kei Eguchi: Investigation, Theoretical analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration. Daigo Nakashima: Investigation, Data curation, Simulation, Experiment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: I am grateful to Akira Shibata for his cooperation in the experiments and useful discussions.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fast battery cell voltage equaliser based on the bi-directional flyback converter
IEEE Trans Transp Electr

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Milas Nikolaos T., Tatakis Emmanuel C. “Fast Battery Cell Voltage Equaliser based on the Bi-directional Flyback Converter.” IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification (2022): doi: 10.1109/TTE.2022.3186520.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bidirectional buck-boost converter using cascaded energy storage modules based on cell voltage equalizers
IEEE Trans Power Electron

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Uno Masatoshi, Cheng Dexiao, Onodera Satoru, Sasama Yuta. (2022) “Bidirectional Buck-Boost Converter Using Cascaded Energy Storage Modules Based on Cell Voltage Equalizers.” IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics (2022): doi: 10.1109/TPEL.2022.3203900.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Switched capacitor system for automatic series battery equalization
Proceedings of APEC 97 - applied power electronics conference, vol. 2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Pascual C., Krein P. T. (1997) “Switched capacitor system for automatic series battery equalization.” Proceedings of APEC 97 - Applied Power Electronics Conference 2 (1997): 848-854, doi: 10.1109/APEC.1997.575744.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Double-tiered switched-capacitor battery charge equalization technique
IEEE Trans Ind Electron

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Baughman Andrew C., Ferdowsi Mehdi. (2008) “Double-Tiered Switched-Capacitor Battery Charge Equalization Technique.” IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics 55.6 (2008): 2277-2285, doi: 10.1109/TIE.2008.918401.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Topology, modeling, and design of switched-capacitor-based cell balancing systems and their balancing exploration
IEEE Trans Power Electron

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ye Yuanmao, Cheng Ka Wai E., Fong Yat Chi, Xue Xiangdang, Lin Jiongkang. (2017) “Topology, Modeling, and Design of Switched-Capacitor-Based Cell Balancing Systems and Their Balancing Exploration.” IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics 32.6 (2017): 4444-4454, doi: 10.1109/TPEL.2016.2584925.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A star-structured switched-capacitor equalizer for series-connected battery strings
2017 IEEE energy conversion congress and exposition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shang Yunlong, Xia Bing, Ku Fei, Zhang Chenghui, Cui Naxin, Wang Chunyu, Mi Chris. (2017) “A star-structured switched-capacitor equalizer for series-connected battery strings.” 2017 IEEE Energy Conversion Congress and Exposition (ECCE) (2017): 2723-2727, doi: 10.1109/ECCE.2017.8096510.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A delta-structured switched-capacitor equalizer for series-connected battery strings
IEEE Trans Power Electron

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shang Yunlong, Zhang Chenghui, Cui Naxin, Mi Chunting Chris. (2019) “A Delta-Structured Switched-Capacitor Equalizer for Series-Connected Battery Strings.” IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics 34.1 (2019): 452-461, doi: 10.1109/TPEL.2018.2826010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis and design of a voltage equalizer based on boost full-bridge inverter and symmetrical voltage multiplier for series-connected batteries
IEEE Trans Veh Technol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang Xiaoguang, Xi Ligen, Gao Zheng, Li Yuqi, Wen Jing. (2020) “Analysis and Design of a Voltage Equalizer Based on Boost Full-Bridge Inverter and Symmetrical Voltage Multiplier for Series-Connected Batteries.” IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology 69.4 (2020): 3828-3840, doi: 10.1109/TVT.2020.2974530.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and analysis of an inductor-less cross-coupled voltage equalizer
Energy Rep

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kei Eguchi, Akira Shibata, Wanglok Do, Farzin Asadi. (2020) “Design and analysis of an inductor-less cross-coupled voltage equalizer.” Energy Reports 6.9 (2020): 1151-1156, doi: 10.1016/j.egyr.2020.11.061.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermoelectric energy harvesting using a single inductor DC/DC converter employing a negative Dickson multiplier
Energy Rep

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kei Eguchi, Daigo Nakashima, Wanglok Do, Takaaki Ishibashi, Farzin Asadi. (2022) “Thermoelectric energy harvesting using a single inductor DC/DC converter employing a negative Dickson multiplier.” Energy Reports 8.1 (2022): 691-698, doi.org/10.1016/j.egyr.2021.11.209.
